steps.description: Set out what must be proved
steps.explanation: It helps to keep the target in view. We will build a bridge from the fundamental identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta\equiv1$ to a difference of two squares, which is exactly $\sec\theta-\tan\theta$ multiplied by $\sec\theta+\tan\theta$.
Every identity in this chapter grows from this single fact, so it is the natural starting point.

steps.description: Divide every term by cos squared theta
steps.explanation: Dividing by $\cos^2\theta$ turns the ratios into the functions we want: $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ is $\tan\theta$ and $\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ is $\sec\theta$.
Each term simplifies: the first is $\tan^2\theta$, the middle is $1$, and the right becomes $\sec^2\theta$. This is the standard identity linking tangent and secant.

steps.description: Rearrange into a difference of squares
steps.explanation: Moving $\tan^2\theta$ across gives a difference of two squares equal to $1$, which is precisely the product we are aiming to create.

steps.description: Factorise the difference of two squares
steps.explanation: Using $a^2-b^2=(a+b)(a-b)$ with $a=\sec\theta$ and $b=\tan\theta$ exposes both factors that appear in the statement we must prove.
We now work on the left-hand side alone and aim to reach the right-hand side, which keeps the proof clean and one-directional.

steps.description: Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate
steps.explanation: Multiplying by $\dfrac{\sec\theta-\tan\theta}{\sec\theta-\tan\theta}$ is multiplying by $1$, so the value is unchanged, but it manufactures the difference of squares in the numerator.

steps.description: Replace the numerator using the factorisation
steps.explanation: The numerator is exactly the product from Step 6, so it collapses to $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta$.

steps.description: Use the identity to simplify the numerator
steps.explanation: From Step 5 the numerator $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta$ equals $1$, leaving a single clean fraction.
This is precisely the expression on the right of the statement, so the left-hand side has been transformed into the right-hand side.
The manipulation divides by $\sec\theta-\tan\theta$, so the identity holds wherever this is non-zero and wherever $\sec\theta$ and $\tan\theta$ are themselves defined.
Testing one convenient angle guards against slips. Both sides give $\sqrt{2}+1$ (rationalising $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2-1}$ by multiplying by $\dfrac{\sqrt2+1}{\sqrt2+1}$), so the identity checks out.
The left-hand side has been rewritten exactly as the right-hand side using only the Pythagorean identity, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Starting from $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta\equiv1$ and factorising, $\sec\theta+\tan\theta=\dfrac{(\sec\theta+\tan\theta)(\sec\theta-\tan\theta)}{\sec\theta-\tan\theta}=\dfrac{1}{\sec\theta-\tan\theta}$, as required.
